data.title: Meme From the sea and back to the sea
data.description:
Pear Blossom·Night Moon·Begonia·Morning Rain

Meme from Singapore Token2049 to the end of Hong Kong Consensus Conference

History is a spiral upward, and the Super Cycle of Meme is the product of time compression

From Murad, an ordinary employee of OK, to Trump, the most powerful leader in history, who resolutely issued coins

Painful or not, PAIN insisted on completing its debut, whether to speculate on coins or not, CZ began to learn to understand Meme, its momentum has been broken. Not only was there no short essay at the Hong Kong Consensus Conference, there was not much summary of BTC and Meme.

Image Source: @MustStopMurad

Last September, Lion City Murad educated the industry with a prophetic attitude. Memecoin Supercycle filled the screens of all practitioners. Some people dug gold, some sold water. Before and after the split of Solana Meme/AI Agent Meme and $TRUMP, DEXX, GMGN, UniversalX Arrived one after another, joined the gold digging shovel sales competition.

Yesterday in Lion City, today in Hong Kong, even if Murad first mentioned Supercycle in July, it has only been about 8 months. The most interesting thing is that PumpFun has become the biggest winner. Not only did it perfectly avoid the conspiracy of Solana and Mile, but it also realized SOL at a high level. If there is no paradigm innovation at the public chain level, PumpFun and Hyperliquid should be the most important innovations in recent years.

Prelude, from Zoo Market to Meme

Memories of the past life - the "Zoo Market" started by Doge. As Murad said, Doge in 2021 and the subsequent Zoo Market were the first combination of Meme and Coin. Before that, the encryption industry was in an absolute combination of "technology and public chain" narratives. People never thought that air coins could be speculated and could exist stably for a long time.

The fate of this life-the end of FTX in 2022 is the real beginning of the Meme industry. From then on, the crypto industry has become a real currency circle. The long-term value cannot be attached to the public chain, and even cannot be attached to the CEXs with American background, which should be the most stable and profitable.People feel nothingness. Not only that, FTX also has a chain effect. DeFi has completely survived the wild surge period, and the market dream rate has been wiped out.

This is the prelude to the collapse of VC coins. The price plunge of Binance on VC in mid-2024 is just the end.

Technology + VC < Meme + Coin.

In the void, the market-to-dream ratio of VC coins is like the unmoored UST. After being educated by the market for two years, everyone began to have high hopes for BTC, hoping that the four-year cycle would work hard to save the "copycat" market, but unfortunately, Wall Street took the lead in taking it into its pocket. The passage of BTC ETF plunged the crypto market into a liquidity crisis, and PVP under this situation became abominable.

In a sense, BTC ran out of the crypto market and successfully boarded the external market with more liquidity and purchasing power. It's just that the crypto industry, which is used to BTC's bottoming, is trapped by the established thinking and thinks that the immortal is here to promote itself, but it didn't expect that the demon hunting team is "coming to collect you". You should know that Dogecoin in 2021 did not destroy the market, because the market's attention was on the two major schools of copycats led by BTC and ETH. Dogecoin and zoos were just branches and miscellaneous streams. Even Musk's shouting orders attracted new leeks from outside the circle to a considerable extent. This is the last wave of new forces entering the circle so far. And with the FTX in 2022 and the regulatory storm in 2023/2024, there has been no large-scale entry into the currency circle for two whole years. The rest are all thousand-year-old foxes. You are familiar with Liao Zhai, and I watch the Spring and Autumn Annals at night. We were all taught by the same master and cannot break the tricks at all.

Image Source: @MustStopMurad

So, Murad grasped the direction of change in market sentiment. He was not a leader. In July, he tweeted, and the exposure was only more than 1,000. However, in September, Token2049 had an exposure of 8 million on Twitter. The consensus of the entire industry that really worked was Murad’s Memecoin Supercycle It is the igniter that created the final consensus of the crypto industry - let's stir up Meme together. In the interlude, Meme blew up VC value coins. With Binance being censored and CZ being imprisoned, VC coins began to seize the last chance to sprint Binance's liquidity, while on the chain, the weakness of ETH L2 made Vitalik and EF the target of public criticism. Here we have to speak up for Vitalik. He is a loyal fighter for European and American values, not an ambitious person who seeks profit from this, so deep down he has no motivation to do evil and reap profits.

But the people around me don’t think so. How can a young, suddenly rich, idealistic organization operate a public chain with a market value of hundreds of billions? There is no precedent in human history. Therefore, Vitalik has no way to accept the weird traffic of Meme. The high cost of Ethereum mainnet is not suitable. Only L2 can fight. In fact, Base has indeed taken on the responsibility of EVM ecosystem to resist Solana Meme.

But unfortunately, Base, which is operated by CEX and has no mainnet token, can’t support itself. I don’t know if other L2s are dizzy or VCs are too generous with money. In normal wars, they are basically onlookers, which is quite strange like the Western Front.

But Solana is different. After FTX, Solana is in a state of survival as a whole. Any opportunity that can give it a glimmer of hope should not be missed, whether it is the existing DEX, DePIN, NFT, or the new PayFi and payment. Regarding this matter, I wrote at the end of 23 yearsSolana's second comeback: high performance, marketing and DePIN.

Then there is naturally no reason to reject Meme. The inherent innovation and mechanism of Pump Fun will be unfolded in the duel between CEX and DEX. Here you only need to understand that it is not a combination of PumpFun and Meme, but a combination of Pump Fun and Meme on Solana.

What is important is never the technology, but the fit. For example, the same is true for BNB Chain’s promotion of Four Meme. It has nothing to do with timing, but only with the boss’s interest.

Image Description: Total Fees for Pump.Fun
Image Source: Dune

Solana created the first wave of Meme, not PumpFun. The founder's pet and DePIN+ mobile phone concepts, whether refurbished or nostalgic, can be brought out again for a wave, but of course it can only be one wave.

The strongest endogenous version: AI Agent Meme

But God blesses Toly, God prefers Solana, and Buddha blesses Jump. The concept of AI Agent has suddenly become popular in the cryptocurrency circle. What's more interesting is that Agent, which focuses on tool rationality and assistant features, has chosen Meme as its habitat. Is this rational?

In the final analysis, Meme, which has no value but has a price, is too suitable for Agent, which has no rationality but only hype. So far, traditional Agent is still in the laboratory stage and has not been widely popularized. This is even more true for Web3 AI Agent, which is modified on the basis of open source products. Otherwise, DeFAI should have been put into production long ago instead of staying at the stage of PPT deceiving VC.

For AI Agent, please refer to:

AI is Crypto's agent - the evolution of AI Agent

Ai16z is the best representative work of this stage, which has grasped the essence of Meme. It has both the high speculation of Web3 and the endorsement of Big Name of Web2. Let's take a look at DeepSeek R1 at home. That is engineering innovation, AI Agent is not, and Grok3 is not suitable.

In the name of time, Trump returned, and in the name of innovation, Ma Dugong took office, and came to the final stage of Meme, the ultimate evolution of celebrity currency - presidential currency.

Variation, AI Agent to the pinnacle of Presidential Coin

How do you evaluate Presidential Coin?

In a nutshell:It is a multiplier for the poor and the young Ps, and a plaything for the rich and the coin issuing groups.

It doesn’t matter whether the Solana coin issuing group is deeply involved in the $TRUMP and Mile coin issuing. The Solana DeFi ecosystem is a classic that magnifies gambling to the extreme. It is more difficult than another stand-alone Hyperliquid. Hyperliquid’s opponent is the aging dYdX, and Solana’s opponent is the entire Ethereum ecosystem.

It can be said that Solana DeFi does not have the ability to manipulate all presidential coins, but the Solana DeFi spirit with Jupiter as the core definitely exists, at least objectively, and subjectively I don’t know or comment.

$TRUMP has not changed Crypto, and Solana’s long-term and short-term routes are openly conspiring.

This is the last chapter of Meme’s months of carnival. From DeepSeek to reciprocal tariffs, Web3 will not endThe article mentioned that the significance of DeepSeek is to interrupt the "American-led AI narrative", and the AI ​​Agent in the currency circle has been shut down, and the liquidity of Meme has also been shut down, because the AI ​​Agent Meme narrative has also lost its own technical foundation.

After DeepSeek, both Google Gemini and Musk Grok3 have emphasized Chain of Thought (CoT) rather than the Agent promoted by OpenAI. This does not mean that CoT is superior to Agent. The two are not concepts of the same dimension at all. The former is an algorithm and the latter is a product form. It’s just that the market’s focus on AI has changed.

Image Source: @TMB_Agent

The clock began to swing back, and life began to go backwards. If you still insist on being optimistic about Meme, you can find many evidences such as CZ’s attention, but one thing is certain, Meme as a way of asset issuance is no longer friendly to ordinary people. Any asset issuance method, including Binance’s vote for PI retro, is tasked with innovating the deep-rooted problems at its birth, but they have no exception and have all changed.

What is really worth learning is Solana's way of survival and growth, seizing every opportunity to create short-term liquidity, and continuously innovating. In the long run, it does PayFi, payment, stablecoins and other difficult but highly profitable things, and does not become mediocre, but Solana is also a plaything owned by a larger currency issuing group.

Conclusion

You can also see the sea when you come from Dandong. Although the Strait of Singapore is rich, the sea breeze in Hong Kong is more historically heavy. It is also a good choice for Meme to end his hurricane here. The young lady of Shang K and the young man in the currency circle all need to go ashore.

Please note that Meme as an asset type will not die. Even NFT has found its place in the currency circle through equity NFT, but OpenSea will disappear.

That's it, in memory of the Meme SuperCycle that gave us happiness. If CZ All in Meme, then write another Make Meme Great Again. It's a pity that Europe will not be great again, and Luce will not be either.

Only see the new people laugh, not the old people cry.

data.title: Behind Solana’s celebrity tokens: a feast for project owners and insider traders
data.description:
Author: M7 Research Source: X, @m7_research

Recently, with Trump issuing $TRUMP on the Solana chain, a wave of celebrity token issuance has been set off. Tokens such as $MELANIA, $RYAN, $ENRON, and $LIBRA have appeared one after another, and the Meteora platform has quickly become the preferred issuance platform for such high-profile projects. These token projects show a striking similarity: extremely high FDV, exaggerated trading volume, and violent price fluctuations. On the surface, these tokens are sought after by the market due to the celebrity effect, but in-depth analysis shows that behind this is actually a set of carefully designed wealth harvesting mechanisms.

Meteora Platform: Innovative Mechanism Becomes a Manipulation Tool

The Meteora platform has gained favor with its DLMM model, which has lower transaction slippage and a flexible liquidity management mechanism. However, these innovative features, which were originally designed to improve capital efficiency, have been improperly exploited and become profit-making tools for project owners and insider traders.

Operation mode of systematic manipulation

In a typical celebrity token issuance, the project party usually takes the following steps:

  • Create tokens in advance

  • Establish a DLMM trading pool on Meteora

  • Inject trading liquidity

It has been observed that project parties often create DLMM pools for tokens and USDC trading pairs in advance and only inject unilateral liquidity. This means that at the opening, a large number of limit sell orders have been preset waiting for liquidity to flow in, and the zero slippage feature of the DLMM trading bin further amplifies the project's profit margin.

Insider Trading Analysis: Precise Timing and System Operation

In the cases of $MELANIA, $ENRON, $LIBRA, etc., insider traders all knew the contract address (CA), trading pool information and opening time in advance. Specifically:

  • $LIBRA created the token on the 14th, and the transaction was not created until 20 minutes before the opening of the market on the 15th https://solscan.io/tx/3vtogCe5Q52iUYbY6CLRTV3RUf2ggSDoAkPtCYpJrvpvdoqx71mJbS5zgwvze7CDHTBzNohbg4eJiFPw5kUnu5Dh

  • $ENRON created the token on January 25th, and the Meteora pool was not established until one hour before the transaction on February 4th https://solscan.io/tx/ydzeZfhtfM4vwU2dH7ALB3acavcNBh5oTSuPgCCBPonoQvm4AbCo5P2Rw4WqyWUvRJj4D3mUN68JzTK1uwqF8Ej

According to GMGN data, nearly $4.5 million of funds poured into $LIBRA in just 2 seconds after it opened.

A trader (address: 8bZsrR5aRHDZYkWPLQoDFZUKsHCTeJ8uqhPnoMn7baG3) took the lead with a single $1.4 million sniper transaction. The account initiated 170 transactions in the opening block and was not successful until the moment the transaction was opened. Considering that there are pools on Meteora that only add unilateral liquidity every day, such a large amount of precise investment is obviously derived from insider information.

Detailed explanation of profit model

This sniper trader adopted a systematic profit strategy:

  1. Quickly exchanged 1.8 million tokens into US$530,000

  2. The remaining tokens were distributed to 8 sub-accounts

Take the largest sub-account (DmzEMt7XHwA1tZM5d1XBGvTFWoUpTLutpR5d8cMxNghs) as an example: 750,000 tokens were sold every 20 seconds, 14 times in a row.

The remaining tokens were added to the $LIBRA-$SOL trading pair as one-sided liquidity, and 5,500 SOL were harvested after only 7 minutes.

Convert all LIBRA and SOL obtained to USDC within 5 hours after the opening, making a profit of 5 million US dollars.

In the end, the sniper account made a total profit of 17 million US dollars through this bulk dumping method.

At the same time, the project party made even more profit. The developer address (DefcyKc4yAjRsCLZjdxWuSUzVohXtLna9g22y3pBCm2z) also used the unilateral liquidity mechanism to add tokens to the Meteora pool, and the fee income alone reached 10 million US dollars.

Market Impact and Warning

Although the $LIBRA case has attracted much attention due to its high attention, $ENRON, $MELANIA and $RYAN all show similar operating modes. Investors have unknowingly fallen into the "celebrity + Meteora + big truck" fund harvesting trap. The high liquidity mechanism of the Meteora platform has been abused by project parties and insider traders, which has seriously overdrawn the liquidity and investor confidence of the cryptocurrency market.

data.title: : SBF tells the political and legal struggle behind bankruptcy in prison
data.description:
Original: New York Sun "Sam Bankman-Fried Speaks To The New York Sun From Prison",

Compiled by: Odaily Planet Daily jk

Foreword

The following is a 45-minute interview with former cryptocurrency billionaire Sam Bankman-Fried by A.R. Hoffman (hereinafter referred to as AR), deputy editor-in-chief of the New York Sun. At the peak of his cryptocurrency exchange FTX, SBF was considered likely to become the richest man in the world.

However, with the collapse of FTX and its affiliate Alameda Research, everything collapsed in an instant. In the end, Bankman-Fried was convicted of fraud and other crimes and sentenced to 25 years in prison without parole. He has appealed the verdict, insisting that he was presumed guilty and that his company was always solvent and that FTX's bankruptcy was handled with major errors.

This interview was conducted at the Manhattan Detention Center. The following is SBF's own words, edited only as necessary. All three interviews were conducted on the evening of Tuesday, February 18, 2025, each lasting 15 minutes and one hour apart in accordance with prison regulations. The conversation covers his views on the current political situation, his hopes for a possible pardon from President Trump, his reflections on the judge who tried him, and his views on the prosecutor in charge of prosecuting him, Danielle Sassoon. It is worth noting that Sassoon recently resigned for refusing to drop the case against New York Mayor Adams.

In addition, SBF also shared his deeper thoughts on his personal experience - from once controlling a huge fortune to finally having nothing.

Interview transcript

AR: Hi, Sam. I'm AR. It's great to talk to you. I know you have a lot to say, especially about the current political environment. There's been a lot of talk about amnesty lately, and your prosecutor, Danielle Sassoon, has been in the news a lot. We don't have much time, so why don't we just get straight to your thoughts? There's definitely a lot going on lately.

Sam Bankman-Fried:As you said, my prosecutor has been in the news recently for clashing with the Trump Administration's Department of Justice (DOJ). The judge I was tried by, Kaplan, was also one of Trump's New York judges, and the current situation involves a confrontation between the Trump Administration's DOJ and the judicial system that the Biden Administration has inherited. The DOJ is going through a major transformation, and the President and his team believe that there has been bias in the judicial system over the past few decades, prosecutorial abuse of power, and even the politicization of the DOJ is increasing.

AR: Do you think your case was part of this political struggle?

Sam Bankman-Fried: I think it was part of it. Obviously, there were a lot of things going on at the same time, and whenever there is a high-profile case, the careers of all the officials involved are affected. I don't feel that my case was heard fairly and impartially, especially with some of the decisions made by the judge during the trial. For example, Judge Kaplan allowed the prosecutor to tell the jury that everyone lost all their money, but in another decision, he did not allow the defense to refute that.

AR: So the court allowed the prosecutor to make that statement, but prohibited you from clarifying the facts. This was obviously one of the key issues in the trial. This is just one example of many judicial issues. So how do you view the current turmoil in the Department of Justice, and in particular the Southern District Attorney's Office? Do you think your case reflects a broader problem of prosecutorial misconduct?

Sam Bankman-Fried:I do think there are some commonalities between my case and the broader issue. One area that's interesting is the sentencing of all the people who pleaded guilty. You had a Republican who pleaded guilty to very few charges—and frankly, I don't think anybody was really guilty, I certainly didn't think he was guilty—and he was sentenced to seven and a half years in prison. That's four times longer than the three other people who pleaded guilty combined. And those three people who pleaded guilty were Democrats. There were clear signs of politicization, including prosecutors threatening his wife that she could be prosecuted for being a Republican candidate for Congress if he didn't do what the Biden Justice Department wanted. That was one of the most straightforward examples of political manipulation.

At the same time, there were many layers to my case. One of them was that people began to discover that I was actually working much more closely with the Republican Party than the public had previously known. I had provided financial support to Republicans and conservative organizations that had not been made public before. AR: Do you think that’s part of the story? The media sometimes reports that you’ve given staggering amounts to Democrats, while giving token amounts or just to maintain a semblance of balance to Republicans. Do you think your political affiliations and leanings have been misunderstood by the public, the government, or others? Sam Bankman-Fried: I think it’s a little bit of both. This is from a federal prison. When I made my first big donation in 2020, I did give to Biden. Part of that was because I didn’t want the Democratic Party to become the party of Bernie Sanders. At the time, I saw myself as center-left, but I no longer see myself that way, and by 2022, my perspective had changed. I spent a lot of time in Washington, D.C., over the next few years working with lawmakers, regulators, and the executive branch on a variety of issues, most of which had to do with cryptocurrency policy. However, as time went on, I became increasingly disappointed and frustrated with the Biden administration and the Democratic Party. I don't know whether it was my position that changed or the Democratic Party's position that changed, but at least in the areas I was most familiar with - especially cryptocurrency policy - the Biden administration's attitude was extremely destructive and even difficult to work with. Frankly speaking, the Republicans were more rational on these issues, so I spent a lot of time in Washington doing my best to hope that even if the Democrats controlled the White House and the House and Senate at the time, we could still work across party lines to prevent the government from imposing harsh policies on the industry.

AR: I want to ask you a question - I'm not sure if you've followed this topic, but it has been widely discussed: the "attitude change" of leaders in the technology industry, such as Zuckerberg and Bezos. These are people you may have come into contact with. If your trial had not happened, do you think you could have stood on the stage of the presidential inauguration? Or to ask it another way, what do you think of this phenomenon? Do you think your story is somehow related to the changes in the current technology culture?

Sam Bankman-Fried:I certainly can’t speak for them, but I think they’ve probably seen what I’ve seen over the last four years. Zuckerberg probably expressed it best — I guess he was hoping for a reasonable, constructive approach to disinformation from the government, but what ended up happening was “disinformation campaigns in the name of countering disinformation” and more like political censorship. I think they’ve experienced similar frustrations working with the Biden administration as many in the crypto industry have experienced when it comes to business and free speech issues.

AR: What do you think of politicians issuing meme tokens? Have you heard of Trump Coin, which Trump issued before his inauguration?

Sam Bankman-Fried: I’ve heard of it, but I don’t know as much about it as I would like, because access to information is very limited in prison for well-known reasons. However, I’m not surprised to see politicians starting to get involved in the digital space. Over time, I expect more and more industries to explore this space, in part because cryptocurrencies are more flexible than the traditional financial system. This is one of the reasons why the industry is growing so rapidly and there is so much innovation. Blockchain infrastructure is more modern, more open, and cheaper to develop, and there is no need to worry about patents, regulatory monopolies, or other barriers to building across regions.

AR: Let me ask you a question - I have spoken to a number of people in the crypto industry and the bankruptcy and liquidation field who have expressed serious concerns about the handling of your case and the bankruptcy of FTX. A lot of these issues are raised in the court documents, but from your perspective, what makes you still hopeful? What makes you frustrated? Obviously, issues like amnesty are at the intersection of politics and law. I'm curious, from your perspective in prison, what are the possible breakthroughs?

Sam Bankman-Fried:The thing that has frustrated me the most so far - but has also given me some hope in some ways recently - is the bankruptcy and liquidation issues.

Banks are in a similar situation, but on a much larger scale. Banks have millions of customers and trillions of dollars in funds. Sometimes, a large number of customers make withdrawal requests in a short period of time, and the bank needs to pay billions of dollars in a short period of time. Banks do not store all their assets in cash, but hold assets such as government bonds and mortgages. Therefore, they will sell assets in exchange for cash to meet the withdrawal needs.

This is called a "bank run", which does put pressure on the bank, but it does not cause customers to lose money. It is simply a liquidity management issue, and the bank needs to adjust its asset structure in a short period of time to meet market demand.

FTX is in a similar situation. Before the crisis broke out in November 2022, our net assets were about $10 billion, and there was another $20 billion in equity value. Then, all of a sudden, there was a "run" - FTX customers began to withdraw billions of dollars from the platform every day. So we took the normal steps a bank would take to deal with a run, and just like you would sell a car for cash, we were selling assets to generate liquidity.

This process is expected to take days to weeks, but we have received multiple offers to accelerate the transaction. This is indeed a large amount of work, but it is a common liquidity management problem in the financial system and should not result in customers being harmed.

However, the situation did not develop according to normal market logic. A law firm took over FTX and did nothing for the next two years except pretend that there was no money in the account.

AR: Sam, do you think you are innocent?

Sam Bankman-Fried:Yes, absolutely. Of course, there are many things I would choose to handle differently, and I made many decisions as CEO. But without a doubt, the biggest mistake I made was not following through in November 2022.

I should not have let Sullivan & Cromwell take over FTX. I should have stood up to them, but I didn't. After that, not only me, but millions of customers have experienced long and painful waits, being wrongly told that their accounts were empty, and only recently began to receive compensation.

AR: I want to ask you a question - the charges against you, from Danielle Sassoon and other prosecutors, are mainly about fraud, greed, and misconduct. For most people, the amount of money you handled is almost unimaginable.

Let me start here: What is your attitude towards money? There has been a lot of reports about your interest in "Effective Altruism" and other causes. But in a nutshell, what does it feel like to have that much money? What are your real thoughts? In the bigger picture, what is your original intention?

Sam Bankman-Fried:It was an incredible amount of wealth, and it came suddenly—in just a few years, I had more money than I ever imagined in my lifetime.

I approach money on two levels. The first is my personal life. From that perspective, my life hasn't really changed much. I live paycheck to paycheck, and while $200,000 a year is a lot, it's not a billion-dollar fortune. I haven't made any drastic changes to my lifestyle, and to be honest, I've never been attracted to the luxury life. I've never understood the appeal of yachts, for example, and it's never been a wise allocation of resources for me.

Instead, I focus on the larger scale of money and its potential impact on the world. I look at this wealth through the lens of effective altruism: Now that I have money to give, where can it have the greatest positive impact?

Some of it goes to global health—helping people who are dying from preventable diseases like malaria and schistosomiasis. Some of it goes to animal welfare and epidemic prevention. And of course, some of it goes to politics. I think these are the areas where large amounts of money can be used effectively—where billions of dollars can really make a real difference.

AR: How have you navigated the ups and downs of the last few years?

Sam Bankman-Fried: Personally…there are a lot of things I don’t like. Money isn’t the most important thing…I miss my freedom the most. But what frustrates me the most is that I had hoped to have a positive impact on the world, and now I can’t.

There are a lot of great causes and charities that were counting on me for the next decade that are no longer getting that money. Not only that, but they’re also being affected and stigmatized by being associated with me. It makes me feel really sad, and I feel really guilty about it.

AR: You were at the forefront of the cryptocurrency industry, which some have even compared to the “Wild West” at the time. Whether it was the intense competition, working with controversial figures, or being part of an industry that was still figuring itself out – did you ever feel frustrated that some people were still moving freely, while you, someone who had once had altruistic ideals, ended up in the situation you are in now?

In other words, a religious person might ask, “Why me?” Do you ever feel unfair or angry?

Sam Bankman-Fried:Obviously, I’m very frustrated about what’s happened to me, but I try not to view other people in a negative way. That way of thinking is neither constructive nor fair.

Ultimately, my misfortune is not their fault, and they don’t deserve to suffer because of my bad luck. Many of them have accomplished quite impressive things in their fields. Yes, we have worked together and we have disagreed, but my failure does not mean that their success is undeserved.

AR:If you were free one day—by whatever means—have you thought about what you would do? Would you want to go back to crypto? Or have you had an “aha moment” that made you think differently about the future? Or is it too far away to imagine?

Sam Bankman-Fried:That’s obviously a very, very distant question. If and when that day comes, I’ll have to think seriously about my future direction.

But for now, I try not to dwell on it because I have no control over it. I still have more than two decades to serve. And, frankly, from the statements made by the prosecutors and the judge in court, it seems that their intention is to prevent me from having a meaningful life after my sentence is completed.

So, to think about what I would do if the situation was different now is like putting the cart before the horse.

AR: What is the one thing you miss the most? Something simple, like a walk, a cup of coffee, or a late night chat with a friend?

Sam Bankman-Fried:The physical aspect of things has never been the most important to me.

Obviously, I miss the conversations with my friends. But even more painful than that is the loss of my freedom of information - not being able to access information from the outside world at any time. I just miss the ability to search for information on the Internet at will.

Apart from that, what I miss the most is the ability to be engaged in meaningful work. Those two things are the most difficult for me to get over. AR: What's the most realistic thing for you to do next? Do you have any expectations for what the next few weeks or months will look like? Does it feel like the same old thing, or is there some kind of narrative progression? Sam Bankman-Fried: It's been pretty monotonous. There's not a lot to do in prison. I'm used to being fully engaged in my work, but there's nothing to engage in here. In the bigger picture, the most important thing that's going to happen next is the outcome of my appeal. I expect oral arguments in the next three to six months. I'm cautiously optimistic, but the odds of overturning any conviction are slim in the federal criminal justice system. So by "cautiously optimistic," I mean I think there's a glimmer of hope. I hope the judges will take a hard, critical look at what's going on. Beyond that, as prisoners, we have very little control over our lives. Most things are not decided by us, but happen to us. For example, there is now a sudden possibility that I may be transferred to another part of the country, and this process may last for days, weeks, or even months. This will obviously disrupt the little routine I have.

AR: I am very interested in the topic of "control". It seems to me that you have always been a - maybe it's a bit cliché to say this - a "disruptor", someone who prefers to build your own kingdom rather than live by the rules set by others. Whether it is in college, the corporate world, or other fields, you always seem to want to do things your own way.

And now you have been thrown into this most stringent and rigid system. I don't have any particularly profound insights, but I just think that the law itself is a huge system.

Sam Bankman-Fried:Yes, this experience is extremely frustrating. And what makes it even more difficult for me is that I see that many people around me are going through similar things.

There are all sorts of different stories here, but one commonality is exactly what you just said - you're thrown into this system and you're completely deprived of agency. It's a dehumanizing place, an environment where there's no choice, no autonomy.

For a lot of people, especially those who can't afford to hire a lawyer, they rely on public defenders. Sometimes they get a good lawyer, but a lot of times they don't. And then they don't even have control over their own case. Even if they want to be involved, their voices are often not really heard. Their defense process is happening around them, and they themselves are like being swept along by the tide of the justice system, regardless of the facts.

AR: How do you make sense of all this? I think you've always been someone who tends to think logically or even mathematically - correct me if I'm wrong. But now you have to face the way the legal system works. Is this transition frustrating for you? Do you feel like these two ways of thinking are completely different, like Alice through the looking glass, entering a completely strange world?

Sam Bankman-Fried:Yes, there are two levels to this.

One, the law itself can sometimes seem bizarre, infuriating, and even illogical.

This doesn't just apply to my case, I see a lot of people here facing extremely ridiculous dilemmas.

Like, can you kidnap someone for six seconds? It sounds like a strange question, but in fact, in half of the country, the answer is "yes" - at least legally speaking. And such a charge can result in a 25-year sentence.

That's just one example. But the other deeper problem is the way the entire criminal justice system works. You find that at some stages, judges are like mini-dictators who control everything, and at other stages, prosecutors have absolute power. If their decisions don't make sense, they really don't make sense - and there is usually no real way to remedy.

There is no competition, and there is no accountability. In a sense, it is a monopolistic system.

If the people in power have no interest in justice on a particular issue, then that's it. Sure, you can appeal, but the process takes years. It may take three years before you can get a "second opinion."

AR: Before all this happened, you were trying to explore how to use your vast resources to have social impact. Do you think you have found a way for wealth to effectively improve the world?

Sam Bankman-Fried:I have some ideas, but I certainly haven't found the "ultimate answer." I don't think anyone has.

There’s still a lot to learn, but at least some of the basic principles are starting to become clear. Some of it is obvious, like paying attention to detail, paying attention to data, paying attention to the actual impact of different causes.

One ​​of the biggest problems is that it’s very hard to scale. You might find an intervention that works really well with a small amount of resources, but if you increase the funding tenfold, the extra 90% might be completely wasted. Sometimes the limiting factor isn’t even funding, but having a really effective team.

The most consistent pattern we found was that the most successful movements, causes, charities, and organizations tend to have a well-functioning team—one with clear goals, strong but thoughtful leadership, and a structure that works together effectively. But that’s rare.

When that’s missing, efficiency breaks down. No matter how much money you throw at it, it won’t solve the underlying problems.

Frankly, I think the same problem exists with government, and we’re seeing it happen in real time.

I don’t know if you’ve been following Doge and what’s happening around it, but this is a real-life debate about “reform vs. revolution” — whether to choose a scalpel or a chainsaw.

Over time, I’ve been leaning more and more toward the “chainsaw” approach in some cases. Some things don’t just need to cut 10% of the redundancy, but 30%, 50%, or even 70%. But of course, after cutting, you have to rebuild it correctly. You can’t just cut everything and leave a blank.

Look at the SEC — the entire crypto industry can’t communicate properly with it. The problem is not whether this agency needs to be a little more efficient, but that it exists entirely to stifle innovation.

And yet, the irony is that it employs a huge staff and accomplishes almost nothing.

AR: Sam, your story is truly extraordinary. But as you say, both your appeal and potential pardon require an exceptional approach to your case. Do you consider yourself a special case? Is your case unique, or do you think it represents a broader problem?

Sam Bankman-Fried:The patterns that I saw in my case were not unique. There were similar issues in many other cases.

But when the media gets involved, when public attention is high, when politics—whether it’s partisan politics or workplace politics—get involved, everything gets amplified. And when the career incentives of law enforcement officers get involved, these patterns not only persist, but they expand and proliferate and become uncontrollable.

So, in some ways, my case is an extreme manifestation of these issues, but it is by no means the only one.

AR: Hey, Sam, sorry I missed the 3pm call - I was helping a fellow inmate with his legal affairs.

Sam Bankman-Fried: That's OK.

AR: One of your core arguments in your appeal seems to be that FTX was solvent at all times, that the firm had assets sufficient to repay all of its customers, and that the eventual collapse was essentially a liquidity crisis. That seems to be the core of not only your appeal, but also your understanding of what went wrong in your case and how you handled it. Can you elaborate on that? Sam Bankman-Fried: I was looking at financial statements and balance sheets regularly, and during the crisis, we made sure to look at what each entity actually held. I still have many of those financial statements and balance sheets from that time. We gave them all to the bankruptcy administrator in case they needed them, but they had no interest in them. I also followed the post-bankruptcy process, and every financial statement—before, during, and after the bankruptcy—showed that FTX was solvent. All of the data showed that our assets always exceeded our liabilities. That fact never changed, and all of us knew it. That was always our most important metric. If that wasn't the case, we would have a real crisis, and we would have been insolvent and had no solution. But the data can be tracked.

If you look at the assets and liabilities of FTX and Alameda as a whole, during the bull run in 2021, our total net asset value was about $40 billion to $50 billion, and FTX had an additional $20 billion to $30 billion in equity value.

Then, the market crashed in the first half of 2022. Even so, we still had about $20 billion in assets for $10 billion in liabilities - a leverage ratio of about 2x. This means that even if there was a "bank run", if all FTX customers suddenly tried to withdraw all their funds, we would be able to sell this $20 billion in assets for cash to meet the withdrawal demand.

On top of this, FTX has an additional $20 billion in equity to back it up. This did not change throughout the crisis and remains true to this day.

To put it in perspective, I think by November 2022, we could have—and in fact were working to—repay all of our customers in full in kind, meaning that they would have received the assets they requested to withdraw, not the converted fiat amount. We were selling investments to accelerate that process, and we had secured billions of dollars in liquidity to make the payments in a matter of days or weeks. Some of those funds have already started arriving.

However, looking at the present moment, many of our customers are understandably frustrated. After all, it’s been two and a half years, and in the first two years, they received next to nothing.

And now, instead of receiving the cryptocurrency they held, they are receiving the equivalent in U.S. dollars at the petition-day price.

I completely understand their frustration. Frankly, the bankruptcy team and the debtors made a very strange decision - they chose early on not to return customer assets as is, but to pay only the USD equivalent. But this was entirely their decision, not that they "had" to do it.

If they had done nothing for the last two years - even if they had just sat there and done nothing - the bankruptcy estate would have an excess value of $40 billion to $60 billion today, depending on whether equity was included and what they could have done with the company.

If all assets were repaid as is, customers would have received their entire crypto assets, not in USD at the filing date price of $17,000 per Bitcoin, but directly in their Bitcoin.

So, reimbursing all customer assets should not be a problem, either in November 2022 or today. And, with the price of crypto markets going up, things should have gotten better - if assets hadn't been consumed unreasonably over the past period of time.

AR: So, what went wrong? Was it that people didn't read the ledger? Do you think it was malicious behavior, or was there a misunderstanding in the messaging?

Sam Bankman-Fried:If the option was to spend the next few days or weeks liquidating assets - which is what is usually done in a bank run: selling investments and assets to meet withdrawals - then this is feasible and can be done quickly. This is what we have been doing and is a direction that can be taken forward.

The other option was the one proposed by Sullivan & Cromwell (S&C): take over the company and file for bankruptcy protection (Chapter 11) for parts of its business. At the time, I didn't know what their specific plan was, but now we've seen the results.

Of course I can’t know exactly what other people are thinking, but judging by their actions… You asked if they misread the financial statements — it’s not just that they misread them, they simply weren’t willing to seriously review any financial documents the company compiled. They simply denied everything because “these data are not credible”, but at the same time, they confidently announced in public that FTX was “hopeless” and even used metaphors like “garbage dump fire” to describe the company’s situation.

They couldn’t have a reasonable basis to come to this conclusion, especially when they completely ignored the existing financial data. Moreover, the data actually showed something different than what they claimed. According to their own statements over the past few years, initially at the end of 2022 or the beginning of 2023, they claimed that the company had only $1 billion in assets. A few months later, they said they had found $5 billion, which later became $7 billion, and grew to $13 billion by the beginning of 2024. By 2025, it's $15 billion. These assets have always been there, they just kept growing after they stopped ignoring them.

AR: Try to help me understand one question: What is the relationship between the allegations about FTX using customer funds and how FTX and Alameda operate? How does this connect to the argument that "FTX has always been profitable"?

Sam Bankman-Fried:That's a good question - frustratingly, we didn't get a chance to explain this at trial. And the prosecution was not willing to delve into this issue at all.

There are two key angles to think about here. First, it needs to be clear whether "lending" actually occurred. That is, were the assets deposited by customers borrowed and used by other customers? Or does the balance of each account simply reflect their actual holdings, such as a customer has 3 Bitcoin and 2 Ethereum in their account, and the exchange actually holds the corresponding 3 Bitcoin and 2 Ethereum in the wallet to match?

For FTX US, it is basically the latter. Most customers only do spot trading, they do not have margin trading or leveraged lending, so there is no negative balance in their accounts. The total assets of these customers are exactly matched with the assets in the FTX US wallet.

But for FTX International, the situation is different. About 80% of the assets are leveraged, that is, these accounts do not simply hold part of Bitcoin or a small balance of US dollars, but involve leveraged trading. For example, a customer might have $500 in Bitcoin and $400 in Ethereum, but these assets are leveraged… When a user deposits $100, then uses leverage to buy $500 in assets, and borrows the remaining $400, the exchange does not hold $500 in assets to represent that account, but only $100. This leveraged trading model accounts for the majority of trading activity on FTX International, both in terms of asset size and trading volume.

So, of course there is lending involved - this is the core functionality of the platform. Users can use margin trading, cross-margin management, deposit any asset as collateral, and then use leverage to trade, whether buying or selling tokens or futures.

AR (Interviewer): Your argument is not about "moral luck" - that is, it is not that everything can be rationalized after the fact because there was enough money in the end. Instead, you are saying that this operating model is the way the platform works, and it was designed that way from the beginning. Sam Bankman-Fried: Yes, that has always been the core operating logic of the platform. Of course, there is another key consideration in this process: solvency. Even if users know that the platform will involve borrowing and re-hypothecation, they still expect the platform to meet its financial obligations. If you deposit collateral and trade, and then close your position later, the platform should be able to reasonably meet your withdrawal request. If an exchange is completely insolvent, then it cannot meet the withdrawal needs of users, and this is ultimately the biggest risk. If solvency is lost, everything will collapse. But on FTX, most transactions involve borrowing and re-hypothecation by default - this is the operating model of the platform. And from the book data, FTX's total assets are always higher than the assets of pure spot accounts. About 20% of the assets on the platform come from users who don't use leverage or futures trading, and those assets are always directly redeemable.

Of course, there are a lot of details involved - these are just rough outlines. If you go deeper, you can also analyze the Terms of Service in detail and how different parts of them apply to different asset types. But from a macro perspective, this is the basic principle.

AR: Do you think the question of solvency is related to "intent"? Does it involve the mens rea requirement of fraud?

Sam Bankman-Fried:I think it is related to some extent.

To explain it another way - if a platform provides lending services, and someone ends up taking and spending too many assets, causing the platform to go completely bankrupt, with liabilities that are 10 times the assets, and not only mismatched assets in the accounts, but also a complete lack of any sellable assets to repay customers, then such behavior will obviously cause others to lose money and is "knowingly bound to cause loss."

However, if this is not the case, then while this does not answer all legal questions, it at least answers a basic question - is there clear theft here, or is this just a contractual dispute about business obligations?
